programs.name: Utah Medicaid Aging Waiver

eligibility.income_limits: Single applicant limit is $1,305/month (effective 3/1/25–2/28/26) or $1,330/month (effective March 2026–Feb 2027); limits updated annually in March and equivalent to 100% Federal Poverty Level (adjusted by state); almost all income counted (Social Security, pensions, IRA payments, wages, etc.); spousal income may be exempt even if living together; no specific table for household size provided, but varies by program formulas[1][3][4][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: $2,000 countable assets for single applicant (bank accounts, retirement accounts, stocks, bonds, CDs, cash); exempt: primary home (equity under $730,000 if applicant/spouse resides or intends to return), one vehicle, household furnishings/appliances, personal effects, burial plots/irrevocable trusts up to $7,000, life insurance ≤$1,500 face value; 60-month look-back rule applies with penalty period for transfers below fair market value[1][3][4].
other_requirements: Utah resident and U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant[3][5].

Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via InterRAI MDS-HC assessment by registered nurse: requires 2 of 3 - (1) significant physical assistance with ≥2 ADLs (toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, eating); (2) poor orientation to time/place/person requiring nursing facility care; (3) medical condition/needs unsafe without waiver services; dementia diagnosis alone insufficient[1][2].

benefits.value: Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) to delay/prevent nursing home placement for those meeting NFLOC but living at home; core: case management (ongoing plan of care); additional: medical services not typically available community-wide (day treatment, lifeline/emergency response, in-home respite care); specific hours/dollars not fixed, tailored via case manager assessment[1][5][6].

methods: Referral starts with local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) for case manager evaluation[6].
Contact Utah Medicaid general: implied via medicaid.utah.gov (no specific phone/URL in results for Aging Waiver)[6].
San Juan County example: local aging services office[5].
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; potential due to waiver slots, but no details.
forms: InterRAI MDS-HC for NFLOC assessment; standard Medicaid application implied, no specific form number[1].
documents: Proof of income (Social Security awards, pension statements, bank statements).
Proof of assets (bank/CD statements, retirement accounts).
Medical records/doctor documentation for NFLOC.
Proof of residency/citizenship.
No full list; consult case manager[1][4].

geography.regional_variations: Administered via local Area Agencies on Aging (AAA); potential provider/case manager differences by region (e.g., San Juan County office); no explicit wait time/office variations detailed[5][6].
offices_or_providers: Local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) for referral/evaluation[6].

gotchas: Income/assets updated annually (March); check current limits as 2026 figure ($1,330) supersedes 2025 ($1,305)[1][4].
60-month look-back penalty for asset transfers[1].
Must live at home (not in institution/assisted living like New Choices Waiver); NFLOC required but dementia alone insufficient[1][2].
Distinguish from New Choices Waiver (for institutionalized or 18-64 disabled/65+ with institutional history)[2].
Special deductions/exemptions vs. regular Medicaid (e.g., spousal income)[5].
programs.data_shape_notes: Tailored services via case management/NFLOC tiers rather than fixed dollars/hours; annual March income adjustments; home-based only (vs. institutional alternatives); AAA regional referral entry points.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No financial requirements or income limits for eligibility determination[2][3]. However, financing is capped by federal and state budgets, and specific income requirements may vary by program and state[4].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits specified in eligibility criteria[3].
other_requirements: Must reside in a PACE-specific service area[1][3]
Must be certified by the state as needing nursing home level of care (NFLOC)[1][3]
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services at time of enrollment[3][5]
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) plan, Medicare prepayment plan, or Medicare prescription drug plan[3]
Cannot be enrolled in hospice services or certain other programs[3]

benefits.value: Comprehensive medical and social services including coordinated care from an interdisciplinary team. Participants never pay deductibles or copayments for any care, medication, or service provided by the PACE team[2]. For dually eligible (Medicare and Medicaid) participants: no deductibles or copayments[1]. For Medicare-only participants: monthly premium required to cover long-term care portion and Part D drug benefits[1].

geography.restrictions: PACE is NOT currently available in Utah[8]. Utah is one of 19 states without a PACE program[8].
geography.regional_variations: Program does not exist in Utah. A feasibility study indicates potential for a PACE program in Salt Lake City area with $18 million investment to serve 300+ seniors[8].

programs.official_source: https://www.medicare.gov/health-drug-plans/health-plans/your-coverage-options/other-medicare-health-plans/PACE (national program information; not available in Utah)
gotchas: PACE is NOT currently available in Utah[8]. Families in Utah cannot enroll in this program.
PACE programs are only available in some states that offer PACE under Medicaid[5].
Once enrolled, the PACE organization becomes the only entity authorized to provide and pay for care[1].
Enrollment is voluntary but changes how participants access all their benefits[1].
Approximately 90% of PACE participants are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid[3]; those with only Medicare must pay monthly premiums[1].
Financing is capped by federal and state budgets, so waiting lists and specific income requirements may apply depending on the program and state[4].
States define 'nursing home level of care' differently, which affects eligibility determination[2].
programs.data_shape_notes: PACE is a national Medicare/Medicaid program with state-by-state availability. Utah does not currently participate. The program is highly targeted to frail elderly (average participant is 76 years old with multiple complex conditions[3]). No income or asset testing for eligibility, but financing constraints may create waitlists. Benefits structure depends on dual vs. single insurance eligibility. Geographic availability is the primary limiting factor for Utah residents.

## Critical Finding for Utah Families

**PACE is not available in Utah.**[8] Utah is one of 19 states without a PACE program.[8] Families seeking similar comprehensive care coordination for elderly relatives in Utah should explore alternative Medicaid waiver programs, such as Utah's **Aging Home and Community Based Waiver** (for those 65+) or **Utah Community Supports Waiver** (for those disabled before age 22).[7]

If PACE becomes available in Utah in the future, the National PACE Association website (npaonline.org) and Medicare.gov will be the authoritative sources for enrollment information.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

income_limits.note: Income limits are based on Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and are updated annually in April. The following are 2026 limits for Utah.

SLMB.requirement: Income exceeds 100% FPL but does not exceed 120% FPL

QI.requirement: Income exceeds 120% FPL but does not exceed 135% FPL

income_limits.income_exclusions: The first $20 of monthly income, the first $65 of wages, half of wages above $65, food stamps, and certain Native American settlement payments are excluded from income calculations.

asset_limits.note: Utah uses federal asset limits for all three programs.
assets_that_do_not_count: Life insurance with cash value below $1,500
Some Native corporation stocks held by Alaska Native people

other_requirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (even if not currently enrolled)
Must be a resident of Utah
For SLMB and QI: Must be receiving Part A Medicare coverage
For QI: Cannot be receiving Medicaid

covers: Medicare Part A premiums (if you don't have premium-free Part A)
Part A and Part B deductibles
Part A and Part B coinsurance
Part A and Part B copayments
QMB.value: Varies by individual Medicare costs; program pays eligible amounts directly to Medicare

SLMB.value: Covers the full Part B premium amount

QI.value: Covers the full Part B premium amount
QI.additional_benefit: Qualifying for QI automatically qualifies you for the Extra Help prescription drug program

methods: Contact Utah Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) Medicaid office
Apply through your state Medicaid agency

contact_information.note: Specific phone numbers and online application URLs for Utah are not provided in available search results. Contact Utah DHHS Medicaid directly for current contact information.

processing_time.QMB: Should not take more than 45 days from the date the Medicaid agency has all information and verification necessary to determine eligibility. Eligibility becomes effective on the first day of the month following the month of approval.
processing_time.SLMB_and_QI: May be retroactive up to three months prior to the date of application if the person is otherwise eligible

waitlist.QI_specific: QI is not an entitlement program. States receive a set amount of federal money to cover QI benefits. When funds are allocated for a calendar year, no new applicants will receive benefits. Eligibility in future calendar years is not guaranteed.

forms: Application forms are available through the state Medicaid agency; specific form names/numbers not provided in search results
documents: Proof of Medicare Part A eligibility or enrollment
Proof of income (recent pay stubs, tax returns, or other income documentation)
Proof of resources/assets
Proof of residency in Utah
Identification

geography.restrictions: Must be a resident of Utah
offices_or_providers: Utah Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) Medicaid office handles all applications statewide

gotchas: QI is NOT an entitlement program — funding is limited and allocated annually. Once funds run out for the calendar year, new applicants cannot enroll, and eligibility is not guaranteed in future years.
For QI specifically: You cannot be receiving Medicaid to qualify. If you're on Medicaid, you may only qualify for QMB or SLMB.
Income limits change every April based on the federal poverty level announcement. Families should verify current limits annually.
SLMB and QI do not issue Medicaid cards. QMB issues a card that either reads 'MEDICARE COST-SHARING ONLY' (if not receiving Medicaid) or looks like a regular Medicaid card.
Processing can take up to 45 days for QMB, but SLMB and QI may be retroactive up to 3 months — meaning you could receive back payments if approved.
The income exclusions ($20 of income, first $65 of wages, etc.) can make a difference in borderline cases; families should calculate carefully.
Asset limits are relatively low ($9,950 for individuals, $14,910 for couples in 2026). Certain assets don't count (home, one vehicle, burial expenses), but savings and investments do count.
programs.data_shape_notes: This program consists of three separate tiers (QMB, SLMB, QI) with progressively higher income limits but different benefit levels. QMB is the most comprehensive (covers premiums and cost-sharing), while SLMB and QI cover only Part B premiums. QI has a critical gotcha: it's not an entitlement program and funding can run out mid-year. Income limits are tied to the federal poverty level and update annually in April. All three programs share the same asset limits and exclusions. Processing timelines differ: QMB has a 45-day maximum, while SLMB and QI can be retroactive. Utah uses federal standards but may have more generous standards in specific areas.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: For households with a member 60+ or disabled (common for elderly), no gross income limit in Utah. Net income must be at or below 100% Federal Poverty Level (FPL). Examples: ~$1,580/month gross for one person (130% FPL reference, but not required); 2025 figures $15,060/year ($1,255/month) for one, $20,440/year ($1,703/month) for two. Deductions include 20% earned income, standard $209 (households 1-3), medical over $35/month for elderly/disabled, shelter/utilities.[1][2][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Households with member 60+ or disabled: $4,500 countable resources (e.g., bank accounts). Exempt: home, vehicles, retirement savings, household goods, life insurance cash value (some states vary).[2][8]
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen.
Reside in Utah.
Net income test after deductions.
SSI recipients often categorically eligible.
ESAP (Elderly Simplified Application Process) for easier application/recertification for elderly/disabled.[4]
No work requirements for seniors 60+.[7]

benefits.value: Monthly benefits loaded on EBT card for food purchases at authorized stores. Maximum: ~$291 for one-person household, ~$535 for two-person (2026; actual amount based on income/deductions, often less).[1]

methods: Online: Utah online application portal (jobs.utah.gov/myckb).
Phone: State SNAP hotline (specific number via jobs.utah.gov or local office).
In-person/mail: Local Department of Workforce Services office.
Telephone interviews often available for elderly.[1][4]
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; varies by office.
documents: ID/proof of age.
Proof of income (Social Security, pension, etc.).
Proof of expenses (rent, utilities, medical over $35/month, bank statements).[1]

geography.regional_variations: Administered locally via Department of Workforce Services offices; possible differences in processing/wait times by region/office, but uniform rules statewide.[1][4]
offices_or_providers: Local Utah Department of Workforce Services offices (find via jobs.utah.gov).

gotchas: No gross income test for elderly/disabled households—many miss this and think they earn too much.[2]
Higher deductions for medical/shelter key for seniors; track out-of-pocket medical >$35/month.[1][5]
Assets up to $4,500 allowed for elderly (higher than $3,000 general); home/vehicles exempt.[2][8]
ESAP simplifies recertification—elderly often qualify.[4]
Include all who buy/prepare food in household.[3]
Social Security/pensions count as income.[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Elderly/disabled exemptions (no gross income test, higher asset limit $4500, ESAP); benefits scale by household size/income/deductions; statewide with local offices.

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program) / HEAT Program in Utah

eligibility.income_limits: Household gross monthly income must be at or below 150% of the Federal Poverty Level[2][4]. For reference: a family of four making $3,750 or less monthly could qualify[4]. Specific income limits by household size from LIHEAP: 1 Person $1,956/month, 2 People $2,644/month, 3 People $3,331/month, 4 People $4,018/month, 5 People $4,707/month, 6 People $5,394/month[1]
eligibility.asset_limits: There is no asset limit for LIHEAP in Utah[1]
other_requirements: Household must be responsible for paying home energy costs[2][3]
Household must have at least one adult (18 years of age or older or emancipated)[2]
Household must contain at least one U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen[2]
Applicant must be the person responsible for the utilities[3]

benefits.value: Heating assistance: $190–$850 per benefit period; Cooling assistance: $190–$850 per benefit period; Crisis assistance (emergency situations like broken furnace or utility shutoff notice): up to $2,000[1]

methods: In-person at local HEAT office[4]
Phone: Contact your local HEAT office (specific numbers vary by region)[4]
Online/mail: Apply through Utah Department of Workforce Services at mydoorway.utah.gov/utility-assistance/[4]
application.waitlist: Applications processed on rolling basis; program year runs October 1 – September 30 or when federal LIHEAP funds are exhausted, whichever comes first[2]
forms: HEAT Program application form (specific form number not provided in search results)
documents: Copy of power and heat bills (recent)[2][4]
Proof of ALL income received by all household members in the month prior to application[2][4]
Proof of any eligible medical expenses, child support, and alimony paid in the month prior to application[2][4]
Proof of disability, if applicable[2][4]

geography.restrictions: Program operates statewide in Utah, though some regional variations exist through different administrative agencies[2][3]
geography.regional_variations: Summit, Utah, and Wasatch counties are served through Mountainland Association of Governments (MAG)[3]. Other regions served through local HEAT offices under Utah Department of Workforce Services[2][4]
offices_or_providers: Mountainland Association of Governments (MAG) for Summit, Utah, and Wasatch counties[3]
Local HEAT offices throughout Utah (contact through mydoorway.utah.gov or call local office)[4]

gotchas: Application deadlines vary by household type: Priority applications (elderly 60+, disabled, children under 6) accepted October 1 – September 30; general public applications accepted November 1 – September 30[2]. Elderly applicants should apply early in the priority window
LIHEAP household definition includes roommates covered by the same utility bill, even if expenses aren't shared—this differs from SNAP household definitions[1]
Crisis assistance is only available for emergencies (broken furnace, utility shutoff notice), not routine assistance[1]
Program funds are limited and distributed on first-come, first-served basis until exhausted each program year[2]
Heating assistance typically available fall/winter; cooling assistance typically available summer; crisis assistance year-round[1]
You must be responsible for paying utilities to qualify—renters whose landlord pays utilities may not qualify[3]
Income limits are based on gross monthly income (before taxes), not net income[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Utah's LIHEAP operates under the state name 'HEAT Program' (Home Energy Assistance Target). Benefits scale by household size and fuel type. The program has a defined program year (October 1 – September 30) with tiered application windows based on household vulnerability (elderly, disabled, young children receive priority). No asset limit exists, making this relatively accessible to asset-rich but income-poor households. Regional administration varies, with some areas served through MAG and others through state offices.

## Key Context for Families with Elderly Loved Ones

**Priority Access**: If your elderly loved one is 60 or older, they receive priority application status beginning October 1st each year[2]. This is critical—applications from elderly households are accepted starting October 1, while general public applications don't begin until November 1[2].

**No Asset Limit Advantage**: Unlike many assistance programs, LIHEAP has no asset limit in Utah[1]. This means an elderly person with savings, a home, or other assets can still qualify based solely on income.

**Income Qualification**: The income threshold is 150% of the Federal Poverty Level[2]. For a single elderly person, the LIHEAP income limit is $1,956/month[1], which provides a clear benchmark for determining eligibility.

**Crisis Assistance Available**: If your elderly loved one faces an immediate threat like a utility shutoff or broken heating system, crisis assistance up to $2,000 is available year-round[1].

**Documentation Burden**: Be prepared with recent utility bills and detailed income documentation from the prior month—this is the most common application requirement[4].

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: Gross annual household income at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). Exact dollar amounts vary by household size and year; check current FPL guidelines via HHS (not specified in sources for 2026). Alternative entry: current HEAT program recipient (requires 150% FPL for some crisis components). Income based on gross monthly without deductions.[1][2][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in sources.
other_requirements: Household must include at least one adult who is a U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen.[1]
Home must have never been weatherized or weatherized more than 15 years ago; requires energy audit to confirm benefit.[2]
For renters: notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement and landlord may pay portion of costs.[1][2]
Proof of ownership or owner approval for renters/homeowners.[3]
Priority to elderly, disabled, households with children under 6, and high energy users.[2][3]

benefits.value: Professional weatherization services including computerized energy audits, advanced diagnostics, cost-effective home improvements (e.g., insulation, sealing, furnace/AC/water heater repairs), safety inspections, and indoor air quality checks. No direct cash; free for homeowners, partial landlord cost for renters. Year-round crisis help for furnaces, AC, water heaters if HEAT-qualified.[1][2][3]

methods: Contact county-specific agency by phone, mail, fax, email, or drop-off (7-8 regional agencies cover Utah).[1][2][3]

Examples: BRAG (Box Elder, Cache, Rich: 95 W. 100 S. #116, Logan, UT 84321; (435) 752-7242); FCAOG (Beaver, Iron, Washington, Garfield, Kane: 2344 West Industry Way #2, Cedar City, UT 84721); MAG (weatherization@mountainland.org or 478 South Geneva Road, Vineyard, UT 84059); Utah Community Action (fax (801) 214-3208; mail to program address).[1][2][3]
application.processing_time: Not specified; application reviewed for eligibility, then prioritized and placed on waiting list.[1][2][3]
application.waitlist: Yes, all approved applicants placed on waiting list based on priority (e.g., seniors, disabled, young children).[1][2][3]
forms: Agency-specific application form (complete fully/legibly); includes Bill History Release Authorization, Declaration/Proof of Ownership.[3][4]
documents: Social Security cards or HEAT approval letter.[3]
Copy of utility bill.[3]
Verification of all earned/unearned income (if not on HEAT).[3]
Proof of ownership or owner approval.[3]
For renters: notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement.[2]

geography.restrictions: Served by 7-8 regional agencies by county/tribal area (e.g., Utah Community Action: Salt Lake, Tooele, Davis, Weber, Morgan).[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Different agencies/providers per county with own contact info, offices, submission methods (mail, fax, email); potential variations in wait times or processing.[1][2][3]
offices_or_providers: BRAG (Bear River AoG: Box Elder, Cache, Rich, Shoshone Tribe).[1]
FCAOG (Five County AoG: Beaver, Iron, Washington, Garfield, Kane, Paiute Tribe).[1]
MAG (Mountainland: unspecified counties).[2]
Utah Community Action (Salt Lake, Tooele, Davis, Weber, Morgan).[3]

gotchas: Must contact specific county agency; not centralized statewide application.[1]
Renters need landlord agreement and possible cost share; notarized form required.[1][2]
Home must pass energy audit and not recently weatherized (within 15 years).[2]
Priority-based waitlist means elderly qualify for precedence but still wait.[2][3]
HEAT recipients get streamlined entry but must match names/citizenship.[3]
U.S. citizen/qualified non-citizen adult required in household.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered via 7-8 county-specific agencies with unique contacts; priority tiers for elderly/disabled/kids; HEAT crossover eligibility; energy audit gatekeeps services.

programs.name: Utah State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries, their families, and caregivers, including those with limited incomes, under age 65 with disabilities, or dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid[1][3][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or tests apply[1][3]
other_requirements: Must be eligible for Medicare (or soon to be), or a family member/caregiver of a Medicare beneficiary[3][6]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one counseling and assistance on Medicare options (Parts A, B, C, D), Medigap, Medicare Savings Programs, Extra Help/Low Income Subsidy, Medicaid applications, appeals, enrollment/disenrollment, long-term care insurance, and fraud prevention via Senior Medicare Patrol; also includes outreach presentations, education at health fairs, and community events[1][2][5][6][8]

methods: Website: https://daas.utah.gov/seniors/ or Utah-specific SHIP site via shiphelp.org[7][9]
In-person: Available statewide through local affiliates like Mountainland Association of Governments (e.g., 586 East 800 North, Orem, UT 84097, 801-229-3819)[6][8]
Face-to-face or telephone interactive sessions via local network[2][5]
application.processing_time: No formal application or processing time; services provided upon contact as counseling is immediate and free[2][3]
forms: No specific forms required for counseling; assistance available for Medicare/Medicaid/Extra Help application forms during sessions[1]
documents: Medicare card, current insurance details, prescription lists, and income info recommended for personalized counseling but not required to start[1][5]

geography.restrictions: None; operates in every county in Utah[6]
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through statewide network of local affiliates (e.g., senior centers, Area Agencies on Aging) for locally focused counseling reflecting regional Medicare options, but core services uniform[5][6]
offices_or_providers: Utah Department of Health & Human Services, Division of Aging and Adult Services; Utah Insurance Department Health Division; local sites like MAG in Orem[6][7][8]

gotchas: Not affiliated with insurance companies—provides unbiased advice only, does not sell plans or provide direct financial aid; no enrollment on-site for all plans (assists with process); contact 3 months before Medicare eligibility for best prep; separate from Medicare Savings Programs it helps apply for[3][4][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; free counseling service statewide via local affiliates; focuses on education and application assistance rather than direct benefits

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (Home-Delivered Meals)

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits or dollar amounts mentioned; some programs may request financial documents but income is not always a factor. Suggested contributions (e.g., $3.50 per meal in Davis County) help expand services but are voluntary.
other_requirements: Homebound (unable to leave home without assistance)
Unable to shop for food, prepare meals, or unable to do so without significant difficulty due to mobility challenges, disability, frailty, or risk
Resident of the program's delivery zone or service area (varies by county/provider)
Do not drive a vehicle (in some areas like Davis County)
Priority given to isolated individuals

benefits.value: Hot, nutritious midday (lunch) meals delivered 5 days per week (Monday-Friday, excluding holidays), providing one-third of recommended daily intake for older adults; includes protein, vegetables, fruit, grains, and milk; low-sodium, heart-healthy; meets special diet requirements; may include weekend meals (delivered Thursday/Friday in some areas); personal safety check-in during delivery.

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (AAA), senior center, or specific county provider (e.g., Davis County: daviscountyutah.gov/health/aging-and-adult-services/home-delivered-meals; Cache County: 435-755-1722; MAG Utah/Summit/Wasatch: 801-229-3802 or 435-654-4920)
In-person assessment by program staff
Phone referral (family can refer loved one)
application.processing_time: Varies; some process within a week, others longer if waitlist exists; assessment scheduled upon request, meals start soon after approval.
application.waitlist: Possible in some programs, leading to longer processing.
forms: No specific form names/numbers; application via assessment and questions on age, health, mobility, dietary needs, emergency contacts.
documents: Proof of age
Proof of residence
Medical information (if applicable)
Financial documents (sometimes, but not always required)
Dietary restrictions details

geography.restrictions: Limited to specific county or provider delivery zones (e.g., Davis County residents only; Cache County; MAG for Utah, Summit, Wasatch Counties).
geography.regional_variations: Operated by local providers/Area Agencies on Aging; variations in exact criteria (e.g., Davis requires no driving), weekend meals availability, suggested contributions, volunteer requirements; rural areas may deliver fewer days with Division approval.
offices_or_providers: Davis County Aging and Adult Services
Cache County Senior Center (435-755-1722)
MAG (Meals on Wheels of Utah & Summit/Wasatch Counties: 801-229-3802)
Local Area Agencies on Aging (find via mealsonwheelsamerica.org/find-meals-and-services)

programs.official_source: https://rules.utah.gov/publicat/code/r510-104/S510-104.htm (Utah Administrative Code R510-104, Nutrition Programs for the Elderly)
gotchas: Must live in specific delivery zone; verify coverage first or seek alternatives.
Homebound status strictly assessed; car ownership or ability to leave home may disqualify.
Client must be home for delivery (notify by 9 AM if absent); door often left unlocked for safety checks.
Suggested contributions voluntary but support program expansion.
No weekend/evening deliveries standard; medical/secondary meals have extra requirements.
Family referrals allowed but eligibility verified directly with applicant.
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by county/provider with local AAAs managing; no statewide income/asset tests; eligibility emphasizes homebound/mobility over finances; delivery zones create geographic patchwork.

programs.name: Utah Caregiver Support Program (UCSP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits or financial eligibility requirements.[1][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program rules or descriptions.[1][2][3]
other_requirements: Family caregiver must be 18 years or older (adult family member or informal unpaid provider).[1][3]

Care receiver: Older individual (60+ years) OR any age with Alzheimer's or related neurological disorder OR older relative (55+) caring for child <18 (not biological/adoptive parent) OR relative (55+, including parents) caring for adult 18-59 with disability, living with the child/adult.[1][3]
Care receiver unable to perform 2+ Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) without substantial assistance OR requires substantial supervision due to cognitive impairment.[4][5]
Caregiver must demonstrate medium-to-high risk score via DAAS-approved Demographic Intake and Screening tool, Assessment, and Caregiver Burden score.[2][4]

Priority given to: (1) Caregivers who are older individuals with greatest social/economic need (low-income prioritized); (2) Caregivers providing care to persons with Alzheimer's/related disorders; (3) Caregivers 60+ caring for persons with mental retardation/developmental disabilities; (4) Grandparents/older relatives 55+ caring for child <18.[2]

benefits.value: Respite (in-home, overnight, companion services), Case Management, Personal Care Aides, Adult Day Care, Information & Referral, Caregiver Support Groups, Training/Education, Counseling, Supplemental Services (e.g., Emergency Response System, grab bars, incontinence supplies), Homemaking, Home Health Aide. Core services (info, assistance, groups, training) available to all; respite/supplemental based on eligibility/stress index. No specific dollar amounts or hours stated; services funded under Older Americans Act, may provide set dollar amounts in some areas.[1][3][4][5][9]

methods: Contact local Aging and Adult Services office (regional, e.g., Salt Lake County, Davis County, Weber/Morgan); phone examples: Salt Lake 801-468-2460, Davis 801-451-3377, Weber/Morgan 801-625-3866.[3][5]
Website: https://magutah.gov/cgsupport/.[1]
Intake and assessment required; no specific online form or statewide mail/in-person detailed beyond local offices.[1][2][3]
application.waitlist: Yes, often has a waiting list due to insufficient funds; applicants maintained on list and served by risk/burden score order.[2][5]
forms: DAAS-approved Demographic Intake and Risk Screening tool, Assessment, and Caregiver Burden score.[2]
documents: Not explicitly listed; prepare for intake screening, assessment, and burden score evaluation (may include proof of caregiver status, care receiver needs in ADLs/cognition).[2][4]

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide via local Aging and Adult Services providers.[1][2]
geography.regional_variations: Administered regionally through local offices (e.g., Salt Lake, Davis, Weber/Morgan Counties have specific contacts); potential differences in wait times, providers, or service availability.[3][5]
offices_or_providers: MAG Utah (state-level): https://magutah.gov/cgsupport/.[1]
Salt Lake County Aging & Adult Services.[3]
Weber/Morgan Counties: 801-625-3866.[5]

gotchas: No income test, but strict functional/cognitive needs for care receiver (2+ ADLs) and caregiver stress/burden score required—not automatic.[2][4][5]
Priority tiers mean highest-need served first; others waitlisted.[2]
Core services open to all caregivers; respite/supplemental only for those meeting criteria.[1][3][4]
Not a paid caregiver program (unpaid family/informal caregivers supported via services, not wages).[1][6]
Regional contacts needed—no single statewide application hotline/form.[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset test; eligibility driven by caregiver burden score, care receiver ADL deficits, and priority tiers; regionally administered with waitlists; distinguishes core (universal) vs. respite/supplemental services.

eligibility.income_limits: Family income must be below 125% of the federal poverty level[2][6]. The search results do not provide specific dollar amounts or household-size tables. Families should contact their local SCSEP office to determine their exact threshold, as poverty guidelines vary by household size and are updated annually by the Department of Health and Human Services. Note: Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI) is excluded from income calculations[4].
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available search results.
other_requirements: Must be unemployed[2][6]
Must be legally eligible to work in the U.S.[1]
Unable to find a job without assistance or after WIOA services[1]
Maximum of 48 months in the program[1]
Must be willing to provide community service and attend required meetings and training[3]
Must be willing to develop a personalized Individual Employment Plan (IEP)[3]
Must be willing to use all available resources for job searches and economic self-sufficiency[3]

benefits.value: Paid on-the-job training at non-profit and government agencies. Participants work an average of 20 hours per week[5][6] and are paid the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage[5][6]. Training assignments typically range from 6 to 12 months[8]. Participants also receive job search assistance, resume help, and interview skills practice[8]. In Utah specifically, SCSEP participants provided 223,406 hours of community service in the program year ending June 30, 2018[1].

geography.regional_variations: Program availability and funding levels vary by region. Some areas may have waitlists while others do not[2]. Utah has partnerships with 100+ agencies statewide that provide training sites[1].

programs.official_source: https://www.dol.gov/agencies/eta/seniors (U.S. Department of Labor) and https://www.utah.gov/pmn/files/488561.pdf (Utah state program information)
gotchas: Income limits are tied to 125% of federal poverty level, which changes annually and varies by household size—families must verify current thresholds with their local office rather than assuming a fixed dollar amount[2][4][6]

SCSEP is not available in all five counties in Utah[1], though the specific counties are not identified

The program is employment-focused rather than needs-based, emphasizing job training and community service over direct financial assistance.

## Additional Context for Families

**How to Find Your Local Office:**
The search results do not provide a centralized directory of Utah SCSEP offices. Families should start by contacting **Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain Inc.**[8], which holds the SCSEP subaward for Utah, or visit the U.S. Department of Labor's Senior Community Service Employment Program website[6] to locate local providers.

**What Makes SCSEP Different:**
Unlike many senior assistance programs, SCSEP is **employment-focused, not needs-based**. It's designed as a bridge to unsubsidized employment[6], not as ongoing financial support. Participants gain real work experience, earn wages, and receive job placement assistance—making it distinct from programs that provide direct cash assistance or services without employment components.

**Current Program Status (as of April 2026):**
SCSEP remains active and operational[2], though funding cycles have created variability in some regions. Families should apply even if they hear about funding pauses, as positions may become available as funding cycles shift[2].

programs._raw:
I need to clarify an important distinction: **the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program is not a benefits program for elderly individuals—it's an advocacy program that serves residents of long-term care facilities.** The search results do not support the structure you've requested because this program doesn't have eligibility requirements, income limits, asset limits, or a traditional application process for recipients.

Here's what the program actually is:

## What This Program Actually Does

The Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program **advocates on behalf of residents already living in nursing homes and assisted living facilities.**[3][4] It does not provide direct services, financial assistance, or healthcare. Instead, ombudsmen:

- Investigate and resolve complaints from residents[9]
- Protect residents' rights[4]
- Educate residents, families, and staff about regulations and resident rights[10]
- Provide free, confidential services[5]

**Services are provided to persons without regard for income and resources, as requested by an individual or on behalf of an individual, and are available to a person attempting to enter a facility.**[3]

## Who Can Access These Services

There are **no eligibility restrictions** based on age, income, or assets.[3] Any resident of a licensed long-term care facility in Utah can request ombudsman services, or family members can request services on their behalf.

## How to Contact

**Regional offices vary by county:**
- Summit, Utah, and Wasatch counties: 801-229-3809 or ombudsman@magutah.org[5]
- Salt Lake County and other regions: Contact through local Area Agencies on Aging[6]
- Statewide program information: daas.utah.gov/long-term-care-ombudsman/[3]

## Important Distinction

If your guide is intended to help families find **benefits or financial assistance** for elderly relatives, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program is not the right fit. This program is specifically for **advocacy and complaint resolution** for people already in facility-based care. Families seeking financial assistance, healthcare services, or eligibility-based programs would need to explore different resources through Utah's Division of Aging and Adult Services.
